question:
5. Let \( a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{n}, \ldots \) be an arbitrary sequence of positive numbers. Show that
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sup \left(\frac{a_{1}+a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}\right)^{n} \geq e
\]
solution:
Solution. We shall show that there are infinitely many integers \( n \) for which
\[
\frac{a_{1}+a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}>1+1 / n .
\]

Our proof is indirect. Suppose it is false. Then for some integer \( k \) and for all \( n \geq k \)
\[
\frac{a_{1}+a_{n+1}}{a_{n}} \leq \frac{n+1}{n}
\]
whence
\[
\frac{a_{n}}{n} \geq \frac{a_{1}}{n+1}+\frac{a_{n+1}}{n+1} .
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{a_{k}}{k} & \geq \frac{a_{1}}{k+1}+\frac{a_{k+1}}{k+1} \geq \frac{a_{1}}{k+1}+\frac{a_{1}}{k+2}+\frac{a_{k+2}}{k+2} \\
& \geq \frac{a_{1}}{k+1}+\frac{a_{1}}{k+2}+\frac{a_{1}}{k+3}+\frac{a_{k+3}}{k+3} .
\end{aligned}
\]

By induction
\[
\frac{a_{k}}{k} \geq a_{1}\left(\sum_{i=k+1}^{p} \frac{1}{i}\right)+\frac{a_{p}}{p}
\]
for any \( p \geq k \). Then
\[
\sum_{i=k+1}^{p} \frac{1}{i} \leq \frac{a_{k}}{k a_{1}} \text { for } p \geq k .
\]

But the harmonic series diverges, so the sums \( \sum_{i=k+1}^{p} 1 / i \) are unbounded. Thus relation (1) cannot hold for all \( n \geq k \), and hence there must be infinitely many integers \( \boldsymbol{n} \) for which
\[
\frac{a_{1}+a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}>1+\frac{1}{n} .
\]

Then
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sup \left(\frac{a_{1}+a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}\right)^{n} \geq \lim _{n-\infty}\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}=e .
\]

Remark. The lower bound \( e \) cannot be improved because there is a sequence such that
\[
\lim \left(\frac{a_{1}+a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}\right)^{n}=e .
\]

Such a sequence is given by
\[
a_{1}=1, \quad a_{n}=n \log n \quad \text { for } n \geq 1 .
\]

For this sequence
\[
\frac{a_{1}+a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}=1+\frac{b_{n}}{n}
\]
where
\[
b_{n}=\frac{1}{\log n}\left(1+n \log \left(\frac{n+1}{n}\right)+\log (n+1)\right) .
\]

Then
\[
\left(\frac{a_{1}+a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}\right)^{n}=\left(1+\frac{b_{n}}{n}\right)^{n} \rightarrow e
\]
since \( b_{n} \rightarrow 1 \) as \( n \rightarrow \infty \).

descriptive_long.question:
5. Let \( firstterm, secondtm, \ldots, nthterm, \ldots \) be an arbitrary sequence of positive numbers. Show that
\[
\lim _{indexvar \rightarrow \infty} \sup \left(\frac{firstterm+nextterm}{nthterm}\right)^{indexvar} \geq e
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. We shall show that there are infinitely many integers \( indexvar \) for which
\[
\frac{firstterm+nextterm}{nthterm}>1+1 / indexvar .
\]

Our proof is indirect. Suppose it is false. Then for some integer \( startidx \) and for all \( indexvar \geq startidx \)
\[
\frac{firstterm+nextterm}{nthterm} \leq \frac{indexvar+1}{indexvar}
\]
whence
\[
\frac{nthterm}{indexvar} \geq \frac{firstterm}{indexvar+1}+\frac{nextterm}{indexvar+1} .
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{kthterm}{startidx} & \geq \frac{firstterm}{startidx+1}+\frac{kplusone}{startidx+1} \geq \frac{firstterm}{startidx+1}+\frac{firstterm}{startidx+2}+\frac{kplustwo}{startidx+2} \\
& \geq \frac{firstterm}{startidx+1}+\frac{firstterm}{startidx+2}+\frac{firstterm}{startidx+3}+\frac{kplusthr}{startidx+3} .
\end{aligned}
\]

By induction
\[
\frac{kthterm}{startidx} \geq firstterm\left(\sum_{loopidx=startidx+1}^{limitidx} \frac{1}{loopidx}\right)+\frac{pthterm}{limitidx}
\]
for any \( limitidx \geq startidx \). Then
\[
\sum_{loopidx=startidx+1}^{limitidx} \frac{1}{loopidx} \leq \frac{kthterm}{startidx\,firstterm} \text { for } limitidx \geq startidx .
\]

But the harmonic series diverges, so the sums \( \sum_{loopidx=startidx+1}^{limitidx} 1 / loopidx \) are unbounded. Thus relation (1) cannot hold for all \( indexvar \geq startidx \), and hence there must be infinitely many integers \( \boldsymbol{indexvar} \) for which
\[
\frac{firstterm+nextterm}{nthterm}>1+\frac{1}{indexvar} .
\]

Then
\[
\lim _{indexvar \rightarrow \infty} \sup \left(\frac{firstterm+nextterm}{nthterm}\right)^{indexvar} \geq \lim _{indexvar \rightarrow \infty}\left(1+\frac{1}{indexvar}\right)^{indexvar}=e .
\]

Remark. The lower bound \( e \) cannot be improved because there is a sequence such that
\[
\lim \left(\frac{firstterm+nextterm}{nthterm}\right)^{indexvar}=e .
\]

Such a sequence is given by
\[
firstterm=1, \quad nthterm=indexvar \log indexvar \quad \text { for } indexvar \geq 1 .
\]

For this sequence
\[
\frac{firstterm+nextterm}{nthterm}=1+\frac{bnseries}{indexvar}
\]
where
\[
bnseries=\frac{1}{\log indexvar}\left(1+indexvar \log \left(\frac{indexvar+1}{indexvar}\right)+\log (indexvar+1)\right) .
\]

Then
\[
\left(\frac{firstterm+nextterm}{nthterm}\right)^{indexvar}=\left(1+\frac{bnseries}{indexvar}\right)^{indexvar} \rightarrow e
\]
since \( bnseries \rightarrow 1 \) as \( indexvar \rightarrow \infty \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
5. Let \( silvercloud, copperfield, \ldots, ambertrail, \ldots \) be an arbitrary sequence of positive numbers. Show that
\[
\lim _{lighthouse \rightarrow \infty} \sup \left(\frac{silvercloud+midnightowl}{ambertrail}\right)^{lighthouse} \geq e
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. We shall show that there are infinitely many integers \( lighthouse \) for which
\[
\frac{silvercloud+midnightowl}{ambertrail}>1+1 / lighthouse .
\]

Our proof is indirect. Suppose it is false. Then for some integer \( riverstone \) and for all \( lighthouse \geq riverstone \)
\[
\frac{silvercloud+midnightowl}{ambertrail} \leq \frac{lighthouse+1}{lighthouse}
\]
whence
\[
\frac{ambertrail}{lighthouse} \geq \frac{silvercloud}{lighthouse+1}+\frac{midnightowl}{lighthouse+1} .
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{duskmirror}{riverstone} & \geq \frac{silvercloud}{riverstone+1}+\frac{moongarden}{riverstone+1} \geq \frac{silvercloud}{riverstone+1}+\frac{silvercloud}{riverstone+2}+\frac{sunparlor}{riverstone+2} \\
& \geq \frac{silvercloud}{riverstone+1}+\frac{silvercloud}{riverstone+2}+\frac{silvercloud}{riverstone+3}+\frac{starcanyon}{riverstone+3} .
\end{aligned}
\]

By induction
\[
\frac{duskmirror}{riverstone} \geq silvercloud\left(\sum_{stargazer=riverstone+1}^{meadowlark} \frac{1}{stargazer}\right)+\frac{mistyharbor}{meadowlark}
\]
for any \( meadowlark \geq riverstone \). Then
\[
\sum_{stargazer=riverstone+1}^{meadowlark} \frac{1}{stargazer} \leq \frac{duskmirror}{riverstone\, silvercloud} \text { for } meadowlark \geq riverstone .
\]

But the harmonic series diverges, so the sums \( \sum_{stargazer=riverstone+1}^{meadowlark} 1 / stargazer \) are unbounded. Thus relation (1) cannot hold for all \( lighthouse \geq riverstone \), and hence there must be infinitely many integers \( \boldsymbol{lighthouse} \) for which
\[
\frac{silvercloud+midnightowl}{ambertrail}>1+\frac{1}{lighthouse} .
\]

Then
\[
\lim _{lighthouse \rightarrow \infty} \sup \left(\frac{silvercloud+midnightowl}{ambertrail}\right)^{lighthouse} \geq \lim _{lighthouse-\infty}\left(1+\frac{1}{lighthouse}\right)^{lighthouse}=e .
\]

Remark. The lower bound \( e \) cannot be improved because there is a sequence such that
\[
\lim \left(\frac{silvercloud+midnightowl}{ambertrail}\right)^{lighthouse}=e .
\]

Such a sequence is given by
\[
silvercloud=1, \quad ambertrail=lighthouse \log lighthouse \quad \text { for } lighthouse \geq 1 .
\]

For this sequence
\[
\frac{silvercloud+midnightowl}{ambertrail}=1+\frac{windwhisper}{lighthouse}
\]
where
\[
windwhisper=\frac{1}{\log lighthouse}\left(1+lighthouse \log \left(\frac{lighthouse+1}{lighthouse}\right)+\log (lighthouse+1)\right) .
\]

Then
\[
\left(\frac{silvercloud+midnightowl}{ambertrail}\right)^{lighthouse}=\left(1+\frac{windwhisper}{lighthouse}\right)^{lighthouse} \rightarrow e
\]
since \( windwhisper \rightarrow 1 \) as \( lighthouse \rightarrow \infty \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
5. Let \( lastterm, lastbutone, \ldots, constantterm, \ldots \) be an arbitrary sequence of positive numbers. Show that
\[
\lim _{constantval \rightarrow \infty} \sup \left(\frac{lastterm+previousitem}{constantterm}\right)^{constantval} \geq e
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. We shall show that there are infinitely many integers \( constantval \) for which
\[
\frac{lastterm+previousitem}{constantterm}>1+1 / constantval .
\]

Our proof is indirect. Suppose it is false. Then for some integer \( immutable \) and for all \( constantval \geq immutable \)
\[
\frac{lastterm+previousitem}{constantterm} \leq \frac{constantval+1}{constantval}
\]
whence
\[
\frac{constantterm}{constantval} \geq \frac{lastterm}{constantval+1}+\frac{previousitem}{constantval+1} .
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{fixedelement}{immutable} & \geq \frac{lastterm}{immutable+1}+\frac{beforeelement}{immutable+1} \geq \frac{lastterm}{immutable+1}+\frac{lastterm}{immutable+2}+\frac{twobehind}{immutable+2} \\
& \geq \frac{lastterm}{immutable+1}+\frac{lastterm}{immutable+2}+\frac{lastterm}{immutable+3}+\frac{threebehind}{immutable+3} .
\end{aligned}
\]

By induction
\[
\frac{fixedelement}{immutable} \geq lastterm\left(\sum_{finalindex=immutable+1}^{steadfast} \frac{1}{finalindex}\right)+\frac{staticentry}{steadfast}
\]
for any \( steadfast \geq immutable \). Then
\[
\sum_{finalindex=immutable+1}^{steadfast} \frac{1}{finalindex} \leq \frac{fixedelement}{immutable\; lastterm} \text { for } steadfast \geq immutable .
\]

But the harmonic series diverges, so the sums \( \sum_{finalindex=immutable+1}^{steadfast} 1 / finalindex \) are unbounded. Thus relation (1) cannot hold for all \( constantval \geq immutable \), and hence there must be infinitely many integers \( \boldsymbol{constantval} \) for which
\[
\frac{lastterm+previousitem}{constantterm}>1+\frac{1}{constantval} .
\]

Then
\[
\lim _{constantval \rightarrow \infty} \sup \left(\frac{lastterm+previousitem}{constantterm}\right)^{constantval} \geq \lim _{constantval-\infty}\left(1+\frac{1}{constantval}\right)^{constantval}=e .
\]

Remark. The lower bound \( e \) cannot be improved because there is a sequence such that
\[
\lim \left(\frac{lastterm+previousitem}{constantterm}\right)^{constantval}=e .
\]

Such a sequence is given by
\[
lastterm=1, \quad constantterm=constantval \log constantval \quad \text { for } constantval \geq 1 .
\]

For this sequence
\[
\frac{lastterm+previousitem}{constantterm}=1+\frac{stagnantseries}{constantval}
\]
where
\[
stagnantseries=\frac{1}{\log constantval}\left(1+constantval \log \left(\frac{constantval+1}{constantval}\right)+\log (constantval+1)\right) .
\]

Then
\[
\left(\frac{lastterm+previousitem}{constantterm}\right)^{constantval}=\left(1+\frac{stagnantseries}{constantval}\right)^{constantval} \rightarrow e
\]
since \( stagnantseries \rightarrow 1 \) as \( constantval \rightarrow \infty \).

garbled_string.question:
5. Let \( sldkfght, cvbnmwer, \ldots, abtrplmz, \ldots \) be an arbitrary sequence of positive numbers. Show that
\[
\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty} \sup \left(\frac{sldkfght+xmvplkqs}{abtrplmz}\right)^{qzxwvtnp} \geq e
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. We shall show that there are infinitely many integers \( qzxwvtnp \) for which
\[
\frac{sldkfght+xmvplkqs}{abtrplmz}>1+1 / qzxwvtnp .
\]

Our proof is indirect. Suppose it is false. Then for some integer \( hjgrksla \) and for all \( qzxwvtnp \geq hjgrksla \)
\[
\frac{sldkfght+xmvplkqs}{abtrplmz} \leq \frac{qzxwvtnp+1}{qzxwvtnp}
\]
whence
\[
\frac{abtrplmz}{qzxwvtnp} \geq \frac{sldkfght}{qzxwvtnp+1}+\frac{xmvplkqs}{qzxwvtnp+1} .
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{oeirutyw}{hjgrksla} & \geq \frac{sldkfght}{hjgrksla+1}+\frac{pqlxvzsd}{hjgrksla+1} \geq \frac{sldkfght}{hjgrksla+1}+\frac{sldkfght}{hjgrksla+2}+\frac{urmnsdjk}{hjgrksla+2} \\
& \geq \frac{sldkfght}{hjgrksla+1}+\frac{sldkfght}{hjgrksla+2}+\frac{sldkfght}{hjgrksla+3}+\frac{jkgfalsn}{hjgrksla+3} .
\end{aligned}
\]

By induction
\[
\frac{oeirutyw}{hjgrksla} \geq sldkfght\left(\sum_{trgsplok=hjgrksla+1}^{mndfqucz} \frac{1}{trgsplok}\right)+\frac{vhwsdfrq}{mndfqucz}
\]
for any \( mndfqucz \geq hjgrksla \). Then
\[
\sum_{trgsplok=hjgrksla+1}^{mndfqucz} \frac{1}{trgsplok} \leq \frac{oeirutyw}{hjgrksla \, sldkfght} \text { for } mndfqucz \geq hjgrksla .
\]

But the harmonic series diverges, so the sums \( \sum_{trgsplok=hjgrksla+1}^{mndfqucz} 1 / trgsplok \) are unbounded. Thus relation (1) cannot hold for all \( qzxwvtnp \geq hjgrksla \), and hence there must be infinitely many integers \( \mathbf{qzxwvtnp} \) for which
\[
\frac{sldkfght+xmvplkqs}{abtrplmz}>1+\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} .
\]

Then
\[
\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty} \sup \left(\frac{sldkfght+xmvplkqs}{abtrplmz}\right)^{qzxwvtnp} \geq \lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty}\left(1+\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}=e .
\]

Remark. The lower bound \( e \) cannot be improved because there is a sequence such that
\[
\lim \left(\frac{sldkfght+xmvplkqs}{abtrplmz}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}=e .
\]

Such a sequence is given by
\[
sldkfght=1, \quad abtrplmz=qzxwvtnp \log qzxwvtnp \quad \text { for } qzxwvtnp \geq 1 .
\]

For this sequence
\[
\frac{sldkfght+xmvplkqs}{abtrplmz}=1+\frac{lqmxncpt}{qzxwvtnp}
\]
where
\[
lqmxncpt=\frac{1}{\log qzxwvtnp}\left(1+qzxwvtnp \log \left(\frac{qzxwvtnp+1}{qzxwvtnp}\right)+\log (qzxwvtnp+1)\right) .
\]

Then
\[
\left(\frac{sldkfght+xmvplkqs}{abtrplmz}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}=\left(1+\frac{lqmxncpt}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp} \rightarrow e
\]
since \( lqmxncpt \rightarrow 1 \) as \( qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let H be an infinite-dimensional separable Hilbert space over \mathbb{R} or \mathbb{C}.  
For every positive integer n let  

  T_n \in  B(H)  

be bounded, self-adjoint and strictly positive (i.e. \langle T_nx,x\rangle  > 0 for every non-zero x \in  H); hence every T_n is invertible.  
Denote by \|\cdot \| the usual operator norm on B(H).

Show that  

  lim sup_{n\to \infty } \|\,T_n^{-1/2}(T_1+T_{n+1})T_n^{-1/2}\,\|^{\,n}  \geq  e.
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0.  Preliminaries.  
Because T_n^{-1/2}(T_1+T_{n+1})T_n^{-1/2} is self-adjoint and strictly positive, its norm equals its largest spectral value.  For such operators we shall repeatedly use the Rayleigh-quotient formula  

  \|S\| = sup_{0\neq x\in H} \langle Sx,x\rangle  / \|x\|^2.                                         (1)

Step 1.  A variational description well suited to the problem.  
Put  

  S_n := T_n^{-1/2}(T_1+T_{n+1})T_n^{-1/2}        (n \geq  1).

By (1) we have  

  \|S_n\| = sup_{0\neq x} \langle (T_1+T_{n+1})x,x\rangle  / \langle T_nx,x\rangle .                (2)

(The equality follows from the change of variable x \mapsto  T_n^{-1/2}x.)

Step 2.  Choosing one good test vector once and for all.  
Because T_1 is bounded and strictly positive, its numerical range

  \rho (x) := \langle T_1x,x\rangle  / \|x\|^2       (x \neq  0)

is contained in (0,\|T_1\|].  Fix a unit vector u \in  H with  

  \langle T_1u,u\rangle  = \lambda  > 0,                                               (3)

where \lambda  can be taken arbitrarily close to \|T_1\| (if T_1 has no eigenvectors take u so that \rho (u) > \|T_1\|-\varepsilon ).

Introduce the positive scalar sequence  

  a_n := \langle T_nu,u\rangle   (n \geq  1).                                       (4)

Because T_n is strictly positive and u\neq 0, every a_n is strictly positive.

Step 3.  Relating \|S_n\| to the scalar sequence (a_n).  
Evaluate the quotient in (2) at the special vector x=u.  We get  

  \|S_n\| \geq  (\langle (T_1+T_{n+1})u,u\rangle )/(\langle T_nu,u\rangle ) = (\lambda +a_{n+1})/a_n.     (5)

Step 4.  A purely scalar lemma (classical).  
For an arbitrary positive sequence (a_n) and any fixed \lambda >0,

  lim sup_{n\to \infty } ((\lambda +a_{n+1})/a_n)^{\,n} \geq  e.                    (6)

Proof of (6) (sketch).  Assume contrariwise that there exists k such that  
(\lambda +a_{n+1})/a_n \leq  1+1/n for all n\geq k.  Writing this as  

  a_n/n \geq  \lambda /(n+1) + a_{n+1}/(n+1)                               (7)

and iterating (7) for n=k,k+1,\ldots ,p-1 yields  

  a_k/k \geq  \lambda \sum _{j=k+1}^{p} 1/j + a_p/p.                         (8)

Letting p\to \infty  contradicts the divergence of the harmonic series.  Hence (7) cannot hold for all large n, so (\lambda +a_{n+1})/a_n > 1+1/n for infinitely many n; taking limsup and letting n\to \infty  gives (6). \blacksquare 

Step 5.  From the scalar estimate back to the operator.  
Combining (5) and (6) we obtain  

  lim sup_{n\to \infty } \|S_n\|^{\,n}
      \geq  lim sup_{n\to \infty } ((\lambda +a_{n+1})/a_n)^{\,n}
      \geq  e.                                                        (9)

Recalling the definition of S_n, (9) is precisely the desired inequality  

  lim sup_{n\to \infty } \|T_n^{-1/2}(T_1+T_{n+1})T_n^{-1/2}\|^{\,n} \geq  e.   \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher-dimensional structure – we moved from a sequence of numbers to a sequence of bounded positive operators on an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space.
2. Advanced tools required – the solution needs the spectral theorem,
the Rayleigh–Ritz variational principle, and norm identities such as (1);
none of these appear in the original scalar problem.
3. Additional abstraction – operator inversion, operator square roots,
and manipulation of quadratic forms are necessary.
4. Non-trivial reduction – the proof must connect the operator inequality to a scalar one via carefully chosen quadratic forms; simple pattern-matching to the original argument no longer suffices.
5. Infinite-dimensional subtleties – possible absence of eigenvectors is handled through approximate eigenvectors, demanding a deeper functional-analytic insight.

All these layers make the enhanced variant substantially more technical and conceptually harder than both the original problem and the current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let H be an infinite-dimensional separable Hilbert space over \mathbb{R} or \mathbb{C}.  
For every positive integer n let  

  T_n \in  B(H)  

be bounded, self-adjoint and strictly positive (i.e. \langle T_nx,x\rangle  > 0 for every non-zero x \in  H); hence every T_n is invertible.  
Denote by \|\cdot \| the usual operator norm on B(H).

Show that  

  lim sup_{n\to \infty } \|\,T_n^{-1/2}(T_1+T_{n+1})T_n^{-1/2}\,\|^{\,n}  \geq  e.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0.  Preliminaries.  
Because T_n^{-1/2}(T_1+T_{n+1})T_n^{-1/2} is self-adjoint and strictly positive, its norm equals its largest spectral value.  For such operators we shall repeatedly use the Rayleigh-quotient formula  

  \|S\| = sup_{0\neq x\in H} \langle Sx,x\rangle  / \|x\|^2.                                         (1)

Step 1.  A variational description well suited to the problem.  
Put  

  S_n := T_n^{-1/2}(T_1+T_{n+1})T_n^{-1/2}        (n \geq  1).

By (1) we have  

  \|S_n\| = sup_{0\neq x} \langle (T_1+T_{n+1})x,x\rangle  / \langle T_nx,x\rangle .                (2)

(The equality follows from the change of variable x \mapsto  T_n^{-1/2}x.)

Step 2.  Choosing one good test vector once and for all.  
Because T_1 is bounded and strictly positive, its numerical range

  \rho (x) := \langle T_1x,x\rangle  / \|x\|^2       (x \neq  0)

is contained in (0,\|T_1\|].  Fix a unit vector u \in  H with  

  \langle T_1u,u\rangle  = \lambda  > 0,                                               (3)

where \lambda  can be taken arbitrarily close to \|T_1\| (if T_1 has no eigenvectors take u so that \rho (u) > \|T_1\|-\varepsilon ).

Introduce the positive scalar sequence  

  a_n := \langle T_nu,u\rangle   (n \geq  1).                                       (4)

Because T_n is strictly positive and u\neq 0, every a_n is strictly positive.

Step 3.  Relating \|S_n\| to the scalar sequence (a_n).  
Evaluate the quotient in (2) at the special vector x=u.  We get  

  \|S_n\| \geq  (\langle (T_1+T_{n+1})u,u\rangle )/(\langle T_nu,u\rangle ) = (\lambda +a_{n+1})/a_n.     (5)

Step 4.  A purely scalar lemma (classical).  
For an arbitrary positive sequence (a_n) and any fixed \lambda >0,

  lim sup_{n\to \infty } ((\lambda +a_{n+1})/a_n)^{\,n} \geq  e.                    (6)

Proof of (6) (sketch).  Assume contrariwise that there exists k such that  
(\lambda +a_{n+1})/a_n \leq  1+1/n for all n\geq k.  Writing this as  

  a_n/n \geq  \lambda /(n+1) + a_{n+1}/(n+1)                               (7)

and iterating (7) for n=k,k+1,\ldots ,p-1 yields  

  a_k/k \geq  \lambda \sum _{j=k+1}^{p} 1/j + a_p/p.                         (8)

Letting p\to \infty  contradicts the divergence of the harmonic series.  Hence (7) cannot hold for all large n, so (\lambda +a_{n+1})/a_n > 1+1/n for infinitely many n; taking limsup and letting n\to \infty  gives (6). \blacksquare 

Step 5.  From the scalar estimate back to the operator.  
Combining (5) and (6) we obtain  

  lim sup_{n\to \infty } \|S_n\|^{\,n}
      \geq  lim sup_{n\to \infty } ((\lambda +a_{n+1})/a_n)^{\,n}
      \geq  e.                                                        (9)

Recalling the definition of S_n, (9) is precisely the desired inequality  

  lim sup_{n\to \infty } \|T_n^{-1/2}(T_1+T_{n+1})T_n^{-1/2}\|^{\,n} \geq  e.   \blacksquare

4. Non-trivial reduction – the proof must connect the operator inequality to a scalar one via carefully chosen quadratic forms; simple pattern-matching to the original argument no longer suffices.
5. Infinite-dimensional subtleties – possible absence of eigenvectors is handled through approximate eigenvectors, demanding a deeper functional-analytic insight.

All these layers make the enhanced variant substantially more technical and conceptually harder than both the original problem and the current kernel variant.
